title: Where is Russia’s place in the world?
gist: Historian Stephen Kotkin argues Russia is, geographically and institutionally, a 'Near Eastern' patrimonial state — never Western despite its European culture — that has only ever prospered when closely tied to Europe, and whose current 'no-limits' partnership with China is a lopsided, self-imposed trap rather than the strategic triumph Putin claims, evidenced by Russia's post-2022 losses of influence from Syria to Central Asia.

themes.theme: Russia as a Near Eastern country

insights.claim: Russia's oldest city, Derbent on the Caspian in the North Caucasus, was re-founded by the Persian Sassanids in the late fifth to early sixth century AD, taken by Muslim Arab armies in the seventh century, and later passed to the Mongols — physical evidence of Russia's deep Near Eastern roots.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: Shows Russia's 'Near Eastern' character is embedded in its oldest urban geography, not rhetorical — useful for reading Russia's Caucasus and Caspian policy as historical continuity rather than opportunism.
insights.quote: Russia’s oldest city today, located in the North Caucasus on the Caspian, is Derbent.
insights.locator: Russia also maintains a long-standing presence on the Caspian Sea

insights.claim: Russia today is only about 15% Muslim — far below Albania (just over 50%) or Kosovo (nearly 100%) — complicating any simple religious definition of 'Near Eastern,' even though geography and governance still place Russia within that category.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: Signals the 'Near Eastern Russia' thesis rests on governance and geography, not demographics — a nuance that matters when extrapolating the framework to other cases.
insights.quote: today’s Russia is only around 15 per cent Muslim
insights.locator: Muslim-majority status is generally the unstated qualification

insights.claim: Muscovy's princes were vassals of the Muslim Golden Horde khan until full independence in 1480; Ivan the Terrible's 1552 conquest of the Kazan Khanate folded Tatar nobility into the Russian state, and Catherine the Great's 1783 defeat of the Crimean Khanate extended this Slav-Tatar amalgam — a dynamic that 'reverberates through the ages' into today's war against Ukraine.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: Frames the Russia-Ukraine war as the latest iteration of a centuries-old pattern of Russia re-absorbing former Golden Horde territories — a historical anchor for judging how repeatable Russian territorial revisionism is.
insights.quote: it, too, reverberates through the ages (through to the current Russian war against Ukraine)
insights.locator: Ivan the Terrible’s conquest of the Kazan Khanate

insights.claim: By the 19th century, following its conquest of Central Asia, tsarist Russia had more speakers of a Turkic language within its borders than the Ottoman Empire itself did.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: A counter-intuitive data point undercutting Russia's self-image as a purely Slavic/European state — relevant context for reading Moscow's claims to civilisational homogeneity.
insights.quote: tsarist Russia had more speakers of a Turkic language than did the Ottomans
insights.locator: By the 19th-century conquest of Central Asia

insights.claim: What makes a state 'Western' is governance — a stable constitutional order, rule of law, protected property and civil liberties — not culture, ethnicity or religion; Japan is institutionally Western despite being culturally non-European, while Russia, despite its European culture and Orthodox Christianity, has never been institutionally Western.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: The article's central analytical lever: Russia risk should be coded by regime type and institutional durability, not by treating it as a fellow European economy.
insights.quote: Governance – not culture, ethnicity, or religion – is decisive
insights.locator: Being Western involves developing a stable constitutional order

insights.claim: Russia's centuries-long push into the Pacific has 'mostly been one of failure': the Russian Far East's population peaked at around 10 million and has since fallen to under 8 million, dwarfed by Korea, northern Japan, or a single province of northern China, while Vladivostok carries negligible economic weight next to San Francisco, LA, Seattle or Vancouver.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: Undercuts any thesis that Russia's 'pivot to Asia' gives it economic heft comparable to a real Pacific power — its actual Pacific footprint is thin and shrinking.
insights.quote: Russia’s story in the Pacific region has mostly been one of failure
insights.locator: Russia is geographically an East Asian country as well

insights.claim: Russia's 2015 intervention to save Bashar al-Assad triggered a 'Russia is back' narrative (per pundit Fyodor Lukyanov), but the outcome was strategic defeat: Russia became part owner of a civil war, and after Turkish-sponsored forces overthrew Assad, was left clinging to Soviet-era bases including the Tartus naval port while scrambling to sustain logistics across the Sahel.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: Rebuts the 'Russia resurgent in the Middle East' narrative — Russia's regional position is now materially weaker than before 2015, relevant to basing-rights and Mediterranean-power risk premia.
insights.quote: The Middle East is a way to showcase that the period of Russia’s absence from the international scene as a first-rate state has ended
insights.locator: When Putin intervened militarily and saved Bashar al-Assad’s regime

insights.claim: Russia's failure to reconquer Ukraine unwittingly helped set the stage for Assad's downfall, and Russia now holds less sway over Ukraine than it did before attempting to conquer it outright.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: Implies the full-scale invasion was strategically self-defeating even by Russia's own influence metrics — relevant to assessing the durability of any post-war Russian sphere of influence.
insights.quote: a failure that unwittingly helped set the stage for Assad’s downfall
insights.locator: Echoes of this Near East-Middle East overreach

insights.claim: Russia has alienated not just NATO/EU aspirants but Kazakhstan, Armenia and even Azerbaijan; Belarus's Lukashenko remains wary of over-dependence on Moscow despite owing his rule to Putin, and the Eurasian Economic Union and Collective Security Treaty Organization have both become 'zombie-like.'
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: Russia's post-Soviet sphere of influence is hollowing out even among nominal allies — relevant to pricing the durability of Russian-led blocs like the EAEU and CSTO.
insights.quote: The Eurasian Economic Union and the Collective Security Treaty Organization have both become zombie-like.
insights.locator: The Kremlin has also alienated Kazakhstan, Armenia

insights.claim: Russia required an Iranian drone lifeline to sustain its war against Ukraine, revealing the hollowness of Moscow's own defense-industrial capacity despite its claims of military self-sufficiency.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: A concrete supply-chain vulnerability: Russia's war effort depends on external inputs, a lever and risk for sanctions and third-party-supplier policy.
insights.quote: Russia required an Iranian drone lifeline for its war against Ukraine
insights.locator: Sanction-evading trade persists on the basis of mutual opportunism

insights.claim: Historian Richard Pipes defined Russian patrimonialism as a regime 'where the rights of sovereignty and those of ownership blend to the point of being indistinguishable, and political power is exercised in the same manner as economic power' — a structure that has persisted from the Romanovs through the Communist party-state to Yeltsin and Putin.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: The best lens for pricing Russian political-economy risk: state assets and policy are extensions of the ruler's household, implying rule-of-law protections for foreign capital remain structurally unreliable regardless of any sanctions relief.
insights.quote: a regime where the rights of sovereignty and those of ownership blend to the point of being indistinguishable, and political power is exercised in the same manner as economic power
insights.locator: Richard Pipes defined patrimonialism, from the Russian case

insights.claim: The author's own coinage 'Trashcanistan' describes pervasive state capture — via both a historical lack of constraints on executive power and officials' self-privatisation of state assets — across Russia, Ukraine, Azerbaijan and Kazakhstan, with the Baltic states as the main exception, producing a symbiosis between the criminal underworld and officialdom.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: A named, transferable framework for screening post-Soviet jurisdictions for state-capture and expropriation risk beyond Russia itself.
insights.quote: Trashcanistan
insights.locator: Years ago I coined the expression ‘Trashcanistan’

insights.claim: Russia's patrimonialism should not be conflated with ordinary corruption: corruption implies identifiable violations of an otherwise rule-governed system, whereas patrimonialism is 'pre-corrupt' — an all-encompassing system with no rule-based baseline to violate.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: Explains why anti-corruption remedies like transparency rules or compliance regimes are unlikely to fix Russian institutional risk — the problem is structural, not a set of violations.
insights.quote: it should be considered pre-corrupt
insights.locator: Often, this patrimonialism is denounced as corruption

insights.claim: The Russia-China 'no-limits' partnership is starkly asymmetric: China accounts for over one-third of Russia's foreign trade while Russia is only about 4% of China's trade (roughly 80% of it discounted energy and minerals with little room to grow), and China's annual FDI into Russia is under $20 billion (under 1% of Russian GDP) versus the nearly $350 billion the EU had invested before 2022.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: The clearest quantifiable evidence that Russia's pivot to China is a dependency, not a partnership of equals — critical for anyone modelling Russian capital access or de-dollarisation narratives.
insights.quote: China accounts for more than one third of Russia’s foreign trade; Russia makes up a microscopic four per cent of China’s trade.
insights.locator: The staggering bilateral imbalance is well known

insights.claim: Russia's own FSB counterintelligence reportedly refers to China internally as 'the enemy,' warning of Chinese espionage targeting Russian military technology, recruitment of officials and scientists, preparations for territorial revisionism, and Arctic ambitions at Russia's expense — while simultaneously instructing field officers not to jeopardise the relationship.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: Undercuts the 'unbreakable Sino-Russian axis' narrative from inside Russia's own security apparatus — a genuine tail risk for anyone pricing long-run Sino-Russian alignment as stable.
insights.quote: secretly referring to China as ‘the enemy’
insights.locator: his security services remain hard at work to counter Chinese espionage

insights.claim: China's arms imports from Russia plunged by two-thirds between 2020-2024 versus the prior five years, and Russia's most sensitive remaining technologies — military jet engines, quiet submarine capability — can only be transferred to China once, after which the leverage is gone.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: Signals a shrinking, finite well of Russian technological leverage over China — bearish for the idea that Russia can indefinitely monetise military-tech transfer to offset Western sanctions.
insights.quote: China’s weapons imports between 2020 and 2024 plummeted by two thirds compared to the five previous years.
insights.locator: Transfer from Russia of its most coveted technologies

insights.claim: Russia has never enjoyed sustained prosperity, even in relative terms, without deep economic and technological ties to Europe and the US — meaning the current subordination to China is a self-imposed strategic trap, not the 'brilliant strategic choice' Putin and his defenders claim.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: The article's investment-relevant bottom line: durable Russian economic recovery is more plausible via eventual re-engagement with Europe than via the China relationship, which structurally caps Russian growth and technological upgrading.
insights.quote: it more resembles a trap, and a self-imposed one
insights.locator: Putin and his defenders depict Russia’s increasing dependence on China

themes.theme: The impossible return to the West

insights.claim: Samuel Huntington's 'Clash of Civilizations' (1996) is read less as an accurate description of the world and more as a script that Russia, Iran and China have deliberately adopted, branding themselves 'civilizational states' claiming greater legitimacy than the West because of their antiquity — a temptation now visible in India and Turkey too.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: A useful lens for reading Moscow/Beijing/Tehran 'multipolarity' rhetoric as strategic self-legitimation rather than neutral description, relevant to interpreting BRICS-adjacent messaging.
insights.quote: all of which cast themselves as ‘civilizational states’, more ancient than the West and therefore ostensibly more legitimate.
insights.locator: Samuel Huntington’s Clash of Civilizations

insights.claim: Russian elites, including Patriarch Kirill, invoke the 13th-century Alexander Nevsky myth — fighting Western crusaders while submitting peacefully to the Mongol khan — to frame accommodation with China as compatible with Russian identity while resistance to the West is existential; this is a 'dangerous reading of history' given Russia's actual centuries of prosperity through European ties without loss of identity.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: Shows the ideological scaffolding Moscow uses to justify the China pivot domestically — useful for reading Kremlin propaganda and gauging how entrenched the anti-Western turn really is.
insights.quote: This is a dangerous reading of history
insights.locator: By implication, today’s accommodation of China

insights.claim: Some Russian elites reportedly harbour a fantasy that a mutually destructive US-China war would 'rescue' Russia's strategic position by dragging both rivals down toward Russia's own weakened level — a fantasy that would not fix Russia's human-capital, innovation, infrastructure or governance deficits, since any surviving competitor would recover from anything short of nuclear annihilation.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: A tail scenario worth flagging for geopolitical-risk modelling: Russian strategic culture may see a US-China war as net-positive for its relative position, relevant to assessing Moscow's incentives around a US-China crisis.
insights.quote: China and the US would bring each other way down toward Russia’s low level
insights.locator: Some Russian elites quietly harbour a fantasy

insights.claim: Russia's estrangement from the West is Putin's own choice — driven by his decisions to blow up relations with Europe and the United States and then blame the West for the rupture — countering the narrative that NATO expansion alone explains Russian belligerence, since even non-NATO neighbours distrust Moscow.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: Directly challenges the 'NATO expansion caused Russian aggression' thesis — relevant for handicapping any negotiated Russia-West settlement, since the driver is regime-survival logic rather than a fixable grievance.
insights.quote: necessitated by his decisions to blow up relations with Europe and the United States, and blame the West for the blowup
insights.locator: Putin and his defenders depict Russia’s increasing dependence on China
